vulnerabilities.message: Potentially unsafe operation without null checks
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use optional chaining (?.) or null checks before property access

attackVector.description: Null pointer access can cause application crashes and denial of service
attackVector.exploitExample: const length = possiblyNullArray.length; // crashes if null

remediation.explanation: Accessing properties without null checks can cause runtime errors

vulnerabilities.message: Catch block without error type checking may mishandle different error types
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add type checking to handle different error types appropriately

attackVector.description: Without error type checking, different types of errors may be handled inappropriately
attackVector.exploitExample: catch (e) { console.log(e.message); } // assumes e has message property
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors from invalid property access
Inappropriate error handling for security exceptions
Loss of type safety in error handling paths

remediation.after: catch (e) { if (e instanceof Error) { console.log(e.message); } else { console.log("Unknown error"); } }
remediation.explanation: TypeScript catch blocks should check error types to maintain type safety

vulnerabilities.message: Unsafe JSON parsing without error handling or validation
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Wrap JSON.parse in try-catch and validate the parsed data structure

attackVector.description: Malformed JSON can cause crashes and enable prototype pollution attacks
attackVector.exploitExample: const data = JSON.parse(untrustedInput); // can crash or pollute prototype
realWorldImpact: Denial of service through parsing errors

remediation.explanation: Unhandled JSON parsing can cause crashes and enable injection attacks

vulnerabilities.message: Type assertion "as any" bypasses type safety
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use specific types or type guards instead of "as any"

attackVector.description: Using "as any" disables TypeScript's type checking, which can mask bugs and make code harder to maintain. While not directly exploitable, it reduces code quality and may hide underlying issues.
attackVector.exploitExample: const data = userInput as any; // Bypasses all type checks

remediation.before:
const data = userInput as any;
data.someMethod(); // No type checking, runtime errors possible
remediation.explanation: Avoid "as any". Use proper type guards (typeof, instanceof), type predicates, or refactor code to use correct types. TypeScript's type system is designed to catch errors at compile time.

vulnerabilities.message: Using "any" type bypasses TypeScript type safety
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use specific types or unknown instead of any for better type safety

attackVector.description: Type safety bypass can lead to unexpected data structures and security vulnerabilities
attackVector.exploitExample: const userData: any = untrustedInput; // bypasses all type checking
realWorldImpact: Injection attacks through unexpected data types

remediation.after: const userData: UserData = response.data; // or unknown if type is unclear
remediation.explanation: The any type disables type checking and can lead to runtime errors

vulnerabilities.message: Potentially unsafe operation without null checks
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use optional chaining (?.) or null checks before property access

attackVector.description: Null pointer access can cause application crashes and denial of service
attackVector.exploitExample: const length = possiblyNullArray.length; // crashes if null

remediation.explanation: Accessing properties without null checks can cause runtime errors

vulnerabilities.message: async function without try-catch may cause unhandled exceptions
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Wrap await calls in try-catch blocks for proper error handling

attackVector.description: Unhandled async exceptions can crash applications and expose sensitive error information
attackVector.exploitExample: async function fetchUser() { await fetch(); } // no error handling
realWorldImpact: Application crashes from unhandled async exceptions
Resource cleanup failures in error scenarios

remediation.before: async function fetchUser() { const data = await fetch(); }
remediation.after: async function fetchUser() { try { const data = await fetch(); } catch (error) { console.error(error); } }
remediation.explanation: Async functions should use try-catch to handle potential exceptions from await calls

vulnerabilities.message: Catch block without error type checking may mishandle different error types
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add type checking to handle different error types appropriately

attackVector.description: Without error type checking, different types of errors may be handled inappropriately
attackVector.exploitExample: catch (e) { console.log(e.message); } // assumes e has message property
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors from invalid property access
Inappropriate error handling for security exceptions
Loss of type safety in error handling paths

remediation.after: catch (e) { if (e instanceof Error) { console.log(e.message); } else { console.log("Unknown error"); } }
remediation.explanation: TypeScript catch blocks should check error types to maintain type safety

vulnerabilities.message: Empty catch block silently ignores exceptions
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add proper error handling, logging, or re-throw the exception

attackVector.description: Silent exception suppression can hide security vulnerabilities and cause unpredictable application behavior
attackVector.exploitExample: try { riskyOperation(); } catch (e) {} // error completely ignored
realWorldImpact: Application state corruption from ignored errors
Type safety bypass through ignored errors

remediation.after: try { operation(); } catch (e) { console.error("Operation failed", e); throw e; }
remediation.explanation: Empty catch blocks prevent error visibility and can hide security-critical failures

vulnerabilities.message: Empty catch block silently ignores exceptions
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add proper error handling, logging, or re-throw the exception

attackVector.description: Silent exception suppression can hide security vulnerabilities and cause unpredictable application behavior
attackVector.exploitExample: try { riskyOperation(); } catch (e) {} // error completely ignored
realWorldImpact: Application state corruption from ignored errors
Type safety bypass through ignored errors

remediation.after: try { operation(); } catch (e) { console.error("Operation failed", e); throw e; }
remediation.explanation: Empty catch blocks prevent error visibility and can hide security-critical failures

vulnerabilities.message: promise without catch handler may cause unhandled rejections
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add .catch() handler or use try-catch with async/await

attackVector.description: Unhandled promise rejections can crash Node.js applications and expose error details
attackVector.exploitExample: fetch("/api/data").then(data => process(data)); // no error handling
realWorldImpact: Service unavailability and denial of service

remediation.explanation: Unhandled promise rejections can crash applications and expose sensitive debugging information

vulnerabilities.message: Asynchronous operation without error handling
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add try/catch or .catch() to prevent crashes
vulnerabilities.owasp: A09:2025 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

attackVector.description: Unhandled promise rejections can crash Node.js processes or leak error messages to users, potentially exposing sensitive information.
attackVector.exploitExample: await fetch("/api/data"); // If fetch fails, uncaught exception crashes the app

remediation.before: const data = await fetch("/api/users"); // No error handling
remediation.explanation: Always wrap async operations in try/catch blocks or use .catch() to handle errors gracefully

vulnerabilities.triageReason: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures + production code

vulnerabilities.message: Math.random() is cryptographically weak
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use crypto.randomBytes() or crypto.getRandomValues()

attackVector.description: Math.random() uses a predictable pseudo-random algorithm, allowing attackers to predict values for session tokens, passwords, or cryptographic keys.
attackVector.exploitExample: const token = Math.random().toString(36); // Predictable, can be guessed

remediation.after:
import crypto from "crypto";
const sessionId = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString("hex");
remediation.explanation: Use Node.js crypto.randomBytes() or Web Crypto API crypto.getRandomValues() for cryptographically secure random values

vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add try/catch or .catch() to prevent crashes
vulnerabilities.owasp: A09:2025 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

attackVector.description: Unhandled promise rejections can crash Node.js processes or leak error messages to users, potentially exposing sensitive information.
attackVector.exploitExample: await fetch("/api/data"); // If fetch fails, uncaught exception crashes the app

remediation.before: const data = await fetch("/api/users"); // No error handling
remediation.explanation: Always wrap async operations in try/catch blocks or use .catch() to handle errors gracefully

vulnerabilities.triageReason: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures + production code

vulnerabilities.message: setTimeout/setInterval with string or code variable executes code like eval()
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use anonymous function: setTimeout(() => {...}, delay)

attackVector.description: Passing a string or variable containing code to setTimeout/setInterval causes it to be evaluated as code, similar to eval().
attackVector.exploitExample: setTimeout("alert(userInput)", 1000) or setTimeout(code, 1000) where code/userInput is attacker-controlled

remediation.explanation: Always pass a function reference or arrow function to setTimeout/setInterval, never a string or variable containing code

vulnerabilities.message: Non-null assertion (!) in security code
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Explicitly check for null/undefined

attackVector.description: Non-null assertion operator (!) bypasses null checks in security-critical code, potentially causing authentication bypasses or crashes.
attackVector.exploitExample: if (user.authToken!) { authenticate(); } // If authToken is null/undefined, it may still proceed incorrectly

remediation.before: const token = getAuthToken()!; // Assumes token exists, crashes if null
remediation.explanation: Always explicitly check for null/undefined in security-critical code. Never use ! operator on authentication/authorization values

vulnerabilities.message: setTimeout/setInterval with string or code variable executes code like eval()
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use anonymous function: setTimeout(() => {...}, delay)

attackVector.description: Passing a string or variable containing code to setTimeout/setInterval causes it to be evaluated as code, similar to eval().
attackVector.exploitExample: setTimeout("alert(userInput)", 1000) or setTimeout(code, 1000) where code/userInput is attacker-controlled

remediation.explanation: Always pass a function reference or arrow function to setTimeout/setInterval, never a string or variable containing code

vulnerabilities.message: Using "any" type bypasses TypeScript type safety
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use specific types or unknown instead of any for better type safety

attackVector.description: Type safety bypass can lead to unexpected data structures and security vulnerabilities
attackVector.exploitExample: const userData: any = untrustedInput; // bypasses all type checking
realWorldImpact: Injection attacks through unexpected data types

remediation.after: const userData: UserData = response.data; // or unknown if type is unclear
remediation.explanation: The any type disables type checking and can lead to runtime errors

vulnerabilities.message: Type assertion "as any" bypasses type safety
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use specific types or type guards instead of "as any"

attackVector.description: Using "as any" disables TypeScript's type checking, which can mask bugs and make code harder to maintain. While not directly exploitable, it reduces code quality and may hide underlying issues.
attackVector.exploitExample: const data = userInput as any; // Bypasses all type checks

remediation.before:
const data = userInput as any;
data.someMethod(); // No type checking, runtime errors possible
remediation.explanation: Avoid "as any". Use proper type guards (typeof, instanceof), type predicates, or refactor code to use correct types. TypeScript's type system is designed to catch errors at compile time.

vulnerabilities.message: Using "any" type bypasses TypeScript type safety
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use specific types or unknown instead of any for better type safety

attackVector.description: Type safety bypass can lead to unexpected data structures and security vulnerabilities
attackVector.exploitExample: const userData: any = untrustedInput; // bypasses all type checking
realWorldImpact: Injection attacks through unexpected data types

remediation.after: const userData: UserData = response.data; // or unknown if type is unclear
remediation.explanation: The any type disables type checking and can lead to runtime errors

vulnerabilities.message: async function without try-catch may cause unhandled exceptions
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Wrap await calls in try-catch blocks for proper error handling

attackVector.description: Unhandled async exceptions can crash applications and expose sensitive error information
attackVector.exploitExample: async function fetchUser() { await fetch(); } // no error handling
realWorldImpact: Application crashes from unhandled async exceptions
Resource cleanup failures in error scenarios

remediation.before: async function fetchUser() { const data = await fetch(); }
remediation.after: async function fetchUser() { try { const data = await fetch(); } catch (error) { console.error(error); } }
remediation.explanation: Async functions should use try-catch to handle potential exceptions from await calls

vulnerabilities.message: Unsafe type assertion without runtime validation
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add runtime type validation before type assertions

attackVector.description: Unsafe type assertions can cause runtime errors with malicious or unexpected data
attackVector.exploitExample: const value = (untrustedInput as string).toUpperCase(); // crashes if not string
realWorldImpact: Unexpected method calls on wrong types

remediation.explanation: Type assertions without validation can cause runtime errors with untrusted data

vulnerabilities.message: Asynchronous operation without error handling
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add try/catch or .catch() to prevent crashes
vulnerabilities.owasp: A09:2025 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

attackVector.description: Unhandled promise rejections can crash Node.js processes or leak error messages to users, potentially exposing sensitive information.
attackVector.exploitExample: await fetch("/api/data"); // If fetch fails, uncaught exception crashes the app

remediation.before: const data = await fetch("/api/users"); // No error handling
remediation.explanation: Always wrap async operations in try/catch blocks or use .catch() to handle errors gracefully

vulnerabilities.triageReason: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures + production code

vulnerabilities.message: AI-generated code detected (MEDIUM confidence): 1 hallucinated method(s) found (.remove)
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Replace hallucinated methods with correct TypeScript equivalents. Found: .remove(). Review and rewrite AI-generated code sections.

attackVector.description: AI-generated code with hallucinated methods creates runtime TypeError exceptions exposing system internals through stack traces. Attackers can trigger these errors repeatedly to map application structure and identify vulnerable endpoints. Inconsistent code patterns make security reviews difficult, allowing real vulnerabilities to hide among AI-generated noise. Over-engineered error handling may leak sensitive information in catch blocks.
attackVector.exploitExample:
User triggers: array.append(item)
Result: TypeError: array.append is not a function
Stack trace reveals: Internal file paths, function names, database schema hints
Attacker uses this to map application architecture and plan targeted attacks.
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors revealing sensitive stack traces with internal paths
Logic bugs in access control or validation code (AI-generated if statements)
Performance degradation from inefficient AI-generated loops and algorithms
Maintenance burden: Developers spend hours debugging AI hallucinations
Hidden security vulnerabilities masked by over-engineered code patterns

remediation.explanation: AI code generators (like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude) can hallucinate non-existent methods or generate over-engineered patterns. This creates reliability issues and potential security vulnerabilities. Hallucinated methods cause runtime errors that expose stack traces with sensitive information. Over-engineered code patterns make security audits difficult, hiding real vulnerabilities. Always verify AI-generated code matches TypeScript specifications and follows security best practices.

vulnerabilities.message: Catch block without error type checking may mishandle different error types
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add type checking to handle different error types appropriately

attackVector.description: Without error type checking, different types of errors may be handled inappropriately
attackVector.exploitExample: catch (e) { console.log(e.message); } // assumes e has message property
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors from invalid property access
Inappropriate error handling for security exceptions
Loss of type safety in error handling paths

remediation.after: catch (e) { if (e instanceof Error) { console.log(e.message); } else { console.log("Unknown error"); } }
remediation.explanation: TypeScript catch blocks should check error types to maintain type safety

vulnerabilities.message: Path traversal vulnerability - unsanitized user input in file path
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate and sanitize file paths - use os.path.basename() and whitelist allowed paths

attackVector.description: String concatenation with user input in file paths allows attackers to access arbitrary files using path traversal sequences like ../ or absolute paths. This can expose sensitive files like /etc/passwd, configuration files, or source code.
attackVector.exploitExample: full_path = "/uploads/" + user_path where user_path = "../../etc/passwd"
realWorldImpact: Arbitrary file read via path traversal (../../etc/passwd)

remediation.after:
import os
def read_file(user_path):
    # Sanitize: Extract only filename (removes path traversal)
    safe_filename = os.path.basename(user_path)
    
    # Validate: Check against whitelist
    allowed_files = ["data.txt", "config.json", "report.pdf"]
    if safe_filename not in allowed_files:
        raise ValueError("File not allowed")
    
    # Safe path construction
    full_path = os.path.join("/var/uploads", safe_filename)
    with open(full_path, "r") as f:
        return f.read()
remediation.explanation: Never concatenate user input directly into file paths. Use os.path.basename() to strip directory components, validate against a whitelist of allowed files, and construct paths with os.path.join(). For additional security, use chroot jails or run file operations in sandboxed environments.

vulnerabilities.triageReason: CVSS 7.5 (high) + Broken Access Control + production code

vulnerabilities.message: Path Traversal vulnerability - unrestricted file access
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use pathlib.Path.resolve() and validate against allowed base directory

attackVector.description: Path traversal allows attackers to access files outside intended directories using "../" sequences, potentially reading sensitive system files.
attackVector.exploitExample: open(user_path)  where user_path = "../../../etc/passwd"

remediation.after:
from pathlib import Path
safe_path = (Path(base_dir) / user_file).resolve()
if not safe_path.is_relative_to(base_dir):
    raise ValueError("Invalid path")
with open(safe_path) as f:
remediation.explanation: Always resolve paths and validate they stay within allowed base directory using Path.resolve() and is_relative_to()

vulnerabilities.triageReason: CVSS 7.5 (high) + Broken Access Control + production code

vulnerabilities.suggestion: Replace hallucinated methods with correct Python equivalents. Found: .add(). Review and rewrite AI-generated code sections.

attackVector.description: AI-generated code with hallucinated methods creates runtime AttributeError exceptions exposing system internals through stack traces. Attackers can trigger these errors repeatedly to map application structure and identify vulnerable endpoints. Inconsistent code patterns (mixing camelCase and snake_case) make security reviews difficult, allowing real vulnerabilities to hide among AI-generated noise. Over-engineered error handling may leak sensitive information in except blocks.
attackVector.exploitExample:
User triggers: items.push(value)
Result: AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'push'
Stack trace reveals: Internal file paths, function names, database schema hints
Attacker uses this to map application architecture and plan targeted attacks.
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors revealing sensitive stack traces with internal paths
Logic bugs in access control or validation code (AI-generated if statements)
Performance degradation from inefficient AI-generated loops and algorithms
Maintenance burden: Developers spend hours debugging AI hallucinations
Hidden security vulnerabilities masked by over-engineered code patterns

remediation.explanation: AI code generators (like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude) can hallucinate non-existent methods or generate over-engineered patterns. This creates reliability issues and potential security vulnerabilities. Hallucinated methods cause AttributeError exceptions that expose stack traces with sensitive information. Over-engineered code patterns make security audits difficult, hiding real vulnerabilities. Always verify AI-generated code matches Python specifications (PEP 8) and follows security best practices.

vulnerabilities.message: Exception caught and silently ignored with pass statement
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add proper error handling, logging, or re-raise the exception

attackVector.description: Silent exception suppression can hide security vulnerabilities and cause unpredictable behavior
attackVector.exploitExample:
except Exception:
    pass  # security errors completely ignored
realWorldImpact: Data integrity issues from ignored validation errors
Audit trail gaps from suppressed security events

remediation.explanation: Silent exception suppression prevents visibility into potentially security-critical failures

vulnerabilities.message: Missing await on potentially async function call
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add await keyword before async function calls

attackVector.description: Calling an async function without await causes the function to return a coroutine object instead of the expected result, leading to silent failures where code appears to run but does nothing.
attackVector.exploitExample: result = fetch_user_data()  # Returns coroutine, not user data

remediation.explanation: Always use await with async functions - missing await causes silent failures
